speaker: interviewer
text: Here with Rose Huppert, tape number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Ms. Huppert, after the Germans invaded Poland in 1939, how did life change in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: It changed dramatically, but systematically, not immediately. You could feel the fear, you could feel the terror, and they grabbed people. First of all, they put families to our home. Our family, because of our-- our-- we had the space, whenever they could they brought in from different places, from different towns. And they add the family to our-- to give up the one room. Then another, the one who lost the husband, the young couple, the [INAUDIBLE], she got another family, her own, her sister with-- two sisters, actually-- with husband and two nieces. And it start to be already a very frightening situation. Matter of fact, my brother, when my mom wanted to save him in '32, so he went in hiding with one of the roommates who we'd been helping to get the diploma in engineering school. He went to [INAUDIBLE] and he spent there a couple weeks, or 10 days, I don't recall how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE]. Now he helped him, my brother helped him to get his diploma, the engineering diploma. And then after, when the war broke out, he found out anyway that he was Jewish. But he was nice, and I think as gratitude, he offered-- he stayed there for a short while. But the situation, then did-- also, the situation got gradually bad. They were not allowed to walk in the streets anymore. Not in the Main Street anyway, and they allowed to make like on a big [? field ?] [? separate ?] [INAUDIBLE] I remember they called it [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made like everybody who ever lived there-- we didn't because we lived in suburb-- but the people who lived in Sosnowiec. So they built a little like a-- a vegetable garden or whatever, and that's how the young people spent their time together-- together for a while, for short period. Everything was a short period.

speaker: interviewee
text: And during the night nobody knew, or during day, what happened next, with our next we've [? been in ?] constant and fear. And then my-- for instance, like I-- I was afraid. Mom was afraid that they grab the children. So I was staying my-- into a customer of ours when she became Volksdeutsche after they took over. She became a German. So I would spend a few nights with her at her house, which later on found out that my mom paid plenty, because I even saw after the war a bedspread that my-- belonged to us. But then my brother came back, and Mom realized that the situation gets so bad, my daddy-- so they took a guide, and they pay 200 zlotys-- that was like $200, but that was a lot of money then, not like today. And that was during the winter, and he-- see, Russia took over one part of Poland, Germany took the other part. So my daddy took them to-- to German-- to Russian border, trying to get them through the border. Once in a while, they open and people smuggle them-- smuggled to the Russian [INAUDIBLE], and that's how my daddy went. Was-- took a long time. He did not take us because he was afraid I was too delicate. They were afraid I wouldn't survive. So Mom remained with-- stayed with me and my sister. We had still the business. They did allow, in the beginning, the business. I forgot to mention, to give a-- gave a concession, like a permit, to open for all the businesspeople together for one store. So in the beginning, for a short time, they allow it. And also before it, this, we got this big-- this concession allowed. We had-- my daddy knew someone also from business, Gentile, who they wanted to get together, because my daddy spoke fluently German. So they went to buy in transaction to make some time German people. And they-- they bought some cattle for $10,000. So my daddy who went with him and they made the transaction. So I don't know if they put deposit. One day, they promised, if they come back, they get whatever they bought. The cattle. Then when Daddy came back, they took the money and beat him up and never got anything. And we didn't know what happened to Daddy for couple days. We couldn't show up. He was-- obviously he was unable to come back. And that started-- the situation started to get very drastic. And that time when-- that was after that, that my daddy decided to take, after he got strong and better, he got-- took my brother and tried to save him from-- they started already talking about concentration camps. There was all kind of rumors, which they did not tell us, but like we could see that something is going on very serious. And that's why Daddy took him, my brother, to Russia. Russia was-- actually that wasn't Russia, belonged to Poland, [INAUDIBLE]. And that's how he met my sister-in-law. He was 19 years old. My sister-in-law was 19, but he looked much more mature. She said she would never have married somebody her age, have to be six, seven years older. He say, yes, I am 26. After the marriage, they found out they not. Anyway, so Daddy told him, I'm going back home. That's how he left the tie there. I'm going back home, you know, we left [INAUDIBLE], Mom. And whoever thought of something horrifying, such an annihilation to the Jewish culture and people, and-- and complete to wipe off something which we never in the world's history ever happened. This kind of-- and this is the 20th century, the civilization. I think, this is civilization, I think that the people have a lot to learn yet, if this-- that could something happen like this. And we didn't, he didn't, nobody expects this kind of tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when Daddy came back, and my daddy told my brother, if it quiets down, don't forget to come back. And there was a neighbor also, one of the youngest of the Schwartzbaum, or one of our tenants, what they didn't live with us, but I mean they had been friends before. So you come with him then, my brother promised. So Russia had a trick, and made a big announcement. Who like to go home to register? So they did. He wanted to show off with his bride. She was very intelligent. And so they registered, and they sent them to Siberia as spies. If you want to go home, you came from there, but otherwise you're not-- you're spying. And we didn't know what happened. So both your-- your brother Josef and his new wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister-in-law, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were sent to Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Siberia, to Irkutsk, [INAUDIBLE], was 60 below zero. We didn't know then what happened, but Daddy went. And he-- I think at the time he found out about the International Red Cross in Geneva, and ask me to write a note, which I did. And so we got responded, this name is registered, is alive. He's alive, but they're in Irkutsk. But I-- but I-- Daddy, matter of fact, taught me how to speak, how to write in Russian. All I remember the P is R. Anyway, that's what he-- and then we try to send him packages as much as we could. It was a horrible experience for them as well. And then for-- we ask for pictures. So my brother-- that's why we asked, made the picture during the beginning, in the beginning of the '40s. And we sent them the picture. I hated it, but now I like it, because that's the only thing I-- it brings back close to mine-- to our home. And that's how he remained there until the end of the war. Meantime, I being home, Daddy came back and start to-- when they give us the concession, they said that's not enough. The people have to work for the Germans in order to stay home. This is safer. Meantime I was in hiding here and there, and then one night I was also-- my sister slept in another place, and I was-- we had like a [INAUDIBLE] had storage, like an outside in the backyard special storage, for certain things, for cold, whatever. So Mom tried to hide me, and covered up, and sit all night through to watch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you hiding from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Germans, because they came during-- the Nazis, because they came unexpected during the night, wherever they expected Jews, wherever area they knew that Jews are, look there. They came to raid, and raid, and grabbed them to-- to concentration camps, or who knows. Nobody ever knew. They never know where to turn. So the fear, the terror, was always with us constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember feeling as you were now a 13-, 14-year-old girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a horrifying-- horrifying feeling. I remember one night, Daddy was that time already, I think, he was that time in Russia. Or not. I don't remember at the time. I was with Mom, and we were upstairs. We head from our kitchen upstairs. We had stairs to go up to our master bedroom, to Daddy's bedroom. And Mom was shaking. I was trembling. She could see the Germans at night with the German shepherds, walking around, snooping around, see what they can find. The Jews, some were hiding in the backyard. And I-- we were trembling because we thought every minute they might come in to us, but somehow our thing was confusing, because we had the front door locked up, camouflaging like supposedly we are out of, and nobody's there. And we had this, and the other family was living there, I think. There was a confusion, so I went through with that. And then Daddy, all of a sudden when he went down, they told, if you don't have a certain card, when he's gonna work for the Germans, he would remain home. So he did joined us in town. One day the whole streetcar was surrounded, and he was sent to concentration camp, to Ottmuth Later on he was sent to Markstadt. Now to me, and then everybody was afraid the-- the children they also not safe. So my daddy, my mom, the cousins, they had connection. They had-- they found out all kind of secrets, which they informed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they paid, Mom paid, for machines for me and for my sister. They opened a shop, big, like a factory for the soldiers to make uniforms, and they hired people. They have two shops, one on [INAUDIBLE], Sosnowiec, and one on [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] my sister was employed with, and-- on the [INAUDIBLE], I was. When they gave me the machine and the SS came to see if I can do it, my machine went the other way, so the-- the gruppenfuhrer that means that she was the head of this, our-- how I say [NON-ENGLISH]? Our-- Like, work crew, kind of?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that our room, this flat, flat the [? dentist ?] under certain area, so she knew me. So she said, well, the father is-- isn't away. She tried to explain the situation. And that's what happened. Then I was working from the shop, which we paid off for that. We gave all kind of gifts to the head. They called him [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, from the city, the Jews, they had a [INAUDIBLE] from the Jewish community center. I forget his name, was Marin. Marin. Marin? [? Maren, ?] [? Maren. ?] He had, all the time he had to go back and forth to Berlin, had all the orders how to proceed with the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never forget this moment. It was the first time Daddy wasn't home anymore. There was an announcement that we have all Jews from the city from Sosnowiec you have to gather in one place. It was a big field. To get dressed the best thing we have. It was in summertime, and when I speak I get the goose pimples. Daddy wasn't anymore there at home. And we went to this gathering. There were thousands of people, all Jewish with children, with-- old and young, and everybody was there. And all of a sudden we see, during the day, it was quiet. All of a sudden I see the militia, the Jewish police from the Jewish community center, from the Jewish community. He-- they divided, they made like a chain to separate the people from that. And then I-- and all of a sudden we see a young-- young man, police also, the Jewish police, police-- militia. And he approached us, and he said-- no, then Mom, my [INAUDIBLE], pardon me, Mom she realizes something is going on. So she put down a white, she-- so could see that the people what had the white belt, they were going to the other direction. So she just-- she was a very beautiful lady, and she was strong, so she took the-- she had a lot of courage, she put the white belt. She noticed one of militia was a husband of our dentist, so I think she was a little more secure. And she just tore through the-- the chain, and she went to through there. And they tried to stop her. She said, my husband is there. So they let her through. And then she waved to us that we should come, both of us, I and my sister, so which we did. And coming there, the policeman who show to the-- he was a German, sitting German, SS, whoever they are, by the table. And he took us, the young man, which I later found out was a friend of mine, coincidentally went together to school, to [INAUDIBLE]. So his name was [? Siglund ?]. He just took us around like this, and he said, this is the family. It was I and my sister and the three of us. So the German looked at us and he said [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. We didn't know what [GERMAN] meant. We didn't know that was the start of the whole knowledge, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We didn't know one was to go back home. We had a-- still have the home yet. And the two was evidently left there. We found out was sent to camp, to forced labor. And everything was forced labor, whatever I did, even being home, sort of. So and the third was to [GERMAN]. That means to liquidation. And we then-- we came later on. We heard crying, and people cried. We didn't know what's going on. And our cousins, who helped us to get into the [INAUDIBLE], he himself with the whole family got number three, for destruction, completely, for the Auschwitz. The youngest remain, that he wanted to [GERMAN], that means he wanted to-- he wanted himself to get to the parents. He didn't want to stay alone by himself. He was very heartbroken, actually. And-- but we-- Mom said, don't go. As long as you going to be outside, maybe we can still do something for you-- for the family. Which he did listen, and he paid off someone later on. See, they gather the people, whoever they had, supposed to send away to Auschwitz, for the-- which they probably, you know, they-- they knew. So then they-- he tried-- they gathered in one place, and that I think was [INAUDIBLE]. That was all the people, before they sent to camp, they send them to this building, which they-- wherever they could, whoever they grab who are on their way before they send it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Sosnowiec, this building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And so he paid off someone, and they dig a big hole, because my aunt was quite a heavy person. They dig a big hole, I don't know, probably other people got out maybe through this there too. They got them out, and their apartment the Germans took over, because they lived in very elegant apartment. So the whole building was taken by the Germans. So they were able to get them out that time, for that time being, but even short time was that time important. Every moment we could, we always hoped there would be-- soon the war would be over. Who ever thought of this kind of destruction?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they go out, and-- and that's how we got together. For a while they lived with us, for a short time, until they found a bigger place to live. Another apartment, apartment-- a room somewhere, to-- for temporary life.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was just-- everything, every moment, was just-- everything was temporary. But everybody was hoping that tomorrow would be end of war. Nobody thought that it would be complete, this kind of annihilation of our culture, and life, and-- and freedom, and-- and everything what was considered life, that would be wiped off, wiped out in no time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that time, he went home, and they got back. And then all of a sudden, I-- they put me and my sister, both of us, they had a shop, but they had a-- like a-- from the Jewish community, obviously, they got like a seal, that is taken over. That's nothing there. So they put, to camouflage, they put it outside, that seal. And we had the entrance somewhere. She had the key to be able to go in the shop, in the back of the shop. So I and my sister went, even though we've been working for, they made the shop, they called [INAUDIBLE], which we work for. We were doing this, uniforms, shirts, whatever they need, underwear for the Wehrmacht, for the soldiers, for the war. And she was working one place and I was working. But during the night, we've been able to stay together that night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And meantime, they told my mom there's gonna be a raid again for young girls, but I shouldn't-- we shouldn't be afraid. Our names are not on the list. Meantime, I was with my sister and went home. And all of a sudden, the whole shop was surrounded with SS. And they-- and they hided me, because I had a good voice. And everybody loved to listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who hid you?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the mechanics, which-- what they fixed the machines if something went wrong. So they hided me and two other-- three girls, a sister, I think, and some other, two friends. Four girls were been hidden in this area, in this room, whatever the mechanics had. Where they work. Where they were there before if they needed to be, they helped, and if somebody need to take care of the machines. Anyway, I-- they hided me. And then all of a sudden, somebody must have said something, or was it accidentally. All of a sudden, I see black boots approaching me. And he said, come, come. And I knew I-- I was in. And so that time when they got me surrounded, but they put me and the three girls in separate rooms, because they said that we're gonna be out. And so meantime, we waited and waited. And the-- my cousins' mom find out about this so quickly. She went to the cousins, which they-- they had the connection. So they told, don't worry, you'll be out. And he went to the head of the shop, and he told them to let us out. But the Jewish community said, well, they cannot do it, because they have to wait for the authorities from the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, we got in, and I was sent to the [GERMAN]. Also, this is the place, [INAUDIBLE] where all, whoever they grabbed, was concentrated in this place. And there was-- so I was in it. And after they were-- they taking me to this place, they say, we should have not taken you. You weren't on the list. We say, well, what's the difference? They are [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And on this [INAUDIBLE], they segregated, every time they make an appel and the SS came, and they took out certain group, or whoever they want to. Depends what camp they want to send. Wherever the need was, to different camps, to ladies' camps, or to death camps, or to men's camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your mother and your sister Frania?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, yeah. But when they took me, my mom remained with my sister. And I was in this [GERMAN], they called, so all I saw for the last before I-- that was the last time I saw my mom and my sister and my youngest cousin, when I went to the window, and my mom tried to motion to me I shouldn't cry. And she said she'll come to me. And that was the last time I saw my family, my mom, my sister, and my cousin. And after that, I asked the-- the man who was in charge of this, before they send us to camp, he was in charge of all the Jewish people. The irony of it, that this kind of tragedy, they were still asking for singing. I think that's what gave them the hope, and they knew I was singing. It's strange that people can-- I cannot bring up [INAUDIBLE]. All I do is cry. And that time I was giving, I think, giving strength to them to survive, obviously. Anyway, I asked him if it's gonna be a transport to Daddy, to Markstadt, if he could help me to get to Daddy. He said, look, I cannot do whatever, they-- if I-- if it will be a transport, I will try. So meantime, they called me, called the whole group, whoever was there, all the girls-- girls and women that time. So the SS came, and selected me because I tried to make myself small and [INAUDIBLE]. Meantime was even worse. I didn't realize what I was doing to myself. They probably send me to the worst in Bergen-Belsen, which that was a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why do you think that they would have done that if they thought you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought, as a child, if I'm gonna look delicate, maybe they will feel sorry. They will send me to a men's-- to where they always sent in the men's camp, that the girl works in the kitchen, has that ability more to survive, because she can eat better, and whatever. So I was-- you know, when you hear rumors, you just follow blindly. You never know which way really is right, and which is wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, that time, I-- and then when they selected me, the doctor said no. And I did not understand why-- Darzitski, Dr. Darzitski. For some reason maybe the Jews was-- knew that I was-- that I want to come to my daddy. He tried to take me out of this, and we did that three times. The third time, he says, Rose, there's a max of 60 girls they sending to Markstadt. And I was glad to see that. So when I-- for the first time, I was a first child ever came to a father, to camp, concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came, when Daddy came from work, from [? Bausteller ?], so he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my child, you fell here. You fell in here. And then they made an appel. They all did 120 girls, and then he selected me. The Jewish was [? Burgermeister. ?] He was in charge of the-- there was 3,000 men and 120 girls then, when I was there. And he select 10 girls for the office. So there are nine now, with the 10th [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, [SPEAKING POLISH]. That means in Polish, please, I want to go to the kitchen. Because I knew that everybody was telling them if I go to an office, I wouldn't have anything, because those girls are single, and I knew I had my father there. So I told them the only way I can help is this way. But I used to send my daddy-- [INAUDIBLE]. Excuse me for interrupting, I change the subject. When I was still home, when they grabbed Daddy, we used to-- before even we sent packages to my brother for short time, they were able-- we were able to correspond and send some packages, which we did to help. Some jewelry, something, through which he helped himself in Siberia. And then also with my daddy [INAUDIBLE] after night shift, I tried to walk to [INAUDIBLE], to get some little small-- they allow on the small portion of bread, which I went by foot, and I-- as delicate as I was supposed to be, but somehow was strong then. And I sent for my daddy that sent a small package, because [INAUDIBLE] to walk that far. And Daddy used to get those little packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, shortly when I was there, so I didn't want to go to the office, like after I was-- I regretted, because they had their own room, they had double, double, everything double. And I didn't know then. And no dirty work, just everything clean. But I was naive. I didn't-- but who knew about what to do? So I thought I do a great thing, go to the kitchen. When I was in the kitchen, the German head of the camp, he made an appel, and again he chose me. And I got scared. I didn't know what happened. He said, to the Jew-- to the German kitchen. There was two German woman they used to come every day on the bicycle to help out, some peeling potatoes, whatever they need for the soldiers. So I was privileged to go to this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, when I-- one day I-- trying to get some hot water, I scald my whole leg, from here. Then I tore off the stocking, with-- but-- and the whole skin, I think, it took-- you know, it took off with it, and it was horrible. I was in terrible pain, and I couldn't walk, naturally. And usually they send right away all sick people, or couldn't-- if they couldn't work, they weren't no use for them. They used to send right away, ship them out for-- for [? Never Land. ?] And I, that time, I had a very nice Jewish doctor, with the Jewish-- [? Rena ?], her name was, which was in like in Red Cross, sort of, helping all the sick people. And obviously they kept quiet. But the lagerfuhrer knew that I was-- he allowed me six weeks to be in bed, even. And my daddy came, and they came every day, and they took [INAUDIBLE]. It was not-- whatever medication was available, they tried to alleviate the pain. They took all of the pus every day, and try to help me to heal. It didn't heal so well, but finally I got some relief. And the judenalteste, if they couldn't go to kitchen anymore, he told me want to stay on the [INAUDIBLE]. That means in the barrack. And there was some other things, so until it heals. So after that, I was surprised that they kept me that long, because of-- most the people, they don't, they didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep you in the sick-- in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't the sick. They didn't put me in the sick room. I was in the-- in the room with all the girls. They came to my room and did this all, which was very beautiful. Otherwise, I don't know why. They love me because I used to sing well, and I think the voice was so good, and I-- I don't know what the reason Daddy come. I think everything, people knew us, something. A lot of people didn't know, and for some reason, I-- I remain in the room, and somehow they did not send me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shortly after that, there was a [? rumor-- ?] that if I'm correct, my daddy-- they took away clothes from us, too. Certain things I have from home, yeah, Daddy, my mom, she once-- she got-- we got some correspondence through some friends, who they work in [? Bausteller ?]. And one of us was a neighbor, and-- and through the mothers, and to him-- to a German, obviously, and he gave whatever it was my mom sent. She gave to my-- he gave to my mom, to my father, may he rest in peace. And I stop? Finish your sentence.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Daddy gave it to me. And-- all right?
